title: SEGMENT 9 — Carbamazepine and HLA-B\*1502: Stevens-Johnson syndrome

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Carbamazepine and HLA-B\*1502: Stevens-Johnson syndrome
The next HLA-drug story is even more clinically dramatic, and it's a perfect case study in why pharmacogenomics has to think globally about ancestry.
The drug is carbamazepine.
It's an anticonvulsant — used to treat epilepsy and trigeminal neuralgia, among other conditions.
It's been on the market for over fifty years.
For most patients it's a workhorse drug, effective and reasonably tolerated.
For some patients — fortunately rare — carbamazepine triggers one of the most terrifying adverse drug reactions in all of medicine.
Stevens-Johnson syndrome and its more severe form, toxic epidermal necrolysis, often abbreviated SJS/TEN.
These are immune-mediated reactions in which the skin and mucous membranes essentially undergo massive cell death.
The patient develops a rash that progresses to blisters, then sheets of skin actually slough off the body, including the lining of the mouth, eyes, esophagus, and airway.
The patient ends up looking, and being treated, like a severe burn victim.
Mortality, even with modern intensive care, is around 25% for full toxic epidermal necrolysis.
Survivors often have permanent eye damage, scarring, and chronic pain.
Carbamazepine is one of the most common drug causes of SJS/TEN.
The reaction is rare — maybe 1 in 10,000 prescriptions in unselected populations — but catastrophic when it happens.
In 2004, a Taiwanese research group made a critical observation.
In their patient population, virtually every case of carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN occurred in patients carrying a specific HLA variant: **HLA-B\*1502**.
The association was tight — over 90% of cases were HLA-B\*1502 positive, and the overall risk in carriers was on the order of several hundred-fold elevated relative to non-carriers.
And here's the population genetics.
HLA-B\*1502 is rare in European populations — well under 1%.
It's much more common in many Asian populations: Han Chinese, Thai, Malaysian, Filipino, Indonesian, Vietnamese.
In some of these groups, 10 to 15% of people carry the allele.

So while the overall risk of SJS/TEN with carbamazepine in a European patient is low and somewhat unpredictable, the risk in a Han Chinese patient — driven by the high allele frequency — is substantially higher, and largely concentrated in the genetic carriers.
The FDA, in 2007, issued an alert and updated the carbamazepine label to recommend HLA-B\1502 screening in patients of Asian ancestry before initiating the drug.
In Taiwan, HLA-B\1502 testing before carbamazepine became standard.
A nationwide implementation study showed a substantial reduction in SJS/TEN cases following the policy change.
This is one of the great pharmacogenomics public health victories.

There's a related story for another anticonvulsant, allopurinol — used for gout, but also a major cause of SJS/TEN — and a different HLA allele, HLA-B\*5801, common in Han Chinese and Korean populations.
Same general pattern.
Same general intervention.
These HLA-drug associations have taught the field two lessons.
Lesson one.
HLA variants matter for drug safety in a way that's completely independent of metabolism.
The HLA gene doesn't metabolize anything.
It presents peptides to T cells.
But certain HLA variants, in combination with certain drugs, trigger T-cell responses that cause severe immune-mediated adverse reactions.
It's a different mechanism than CYP, and the testing infrastructure is somewhat different too.
Lesson two — and this one is huge.
Most early pharmacogenomics research was done in European populations.
The HLA-B\*1502/carbamazepine story is a clear case where the European data totally missed the most important association in the disease, because the variant is rare in Europeans.
The risk allele was hiding in plain sight in Asian populations the entire time.
If we had only studied Europeans, we would have, and in fact for years did, miss this association — and the people who paid the price were Asian patients.
We'll return to this theme in Segment 16, because it's one of the central equity problems in modern pharmacogenomics.
That's where we'll take our break.

In the first half of the lecture we've covered why people respond to drugs differently, the CYP450 enzymes that handle most of those drugs, the star allele system for naming variants, the metabolizer phenotypes that variants translate into, and four worked examples — warfarin, clopidogrel, codeine, abacavir, and carbamazepine — that show how genetic variation translates into clinical decisions about dose, drug choice, or whether to prescribe at all.

When we come back, we'll talk about the chemotherapies where pharmacogenomics is now mandatory, the global infrastructure that makes pharmacogenomics implementable, the implementation problem itself, somatic versus germline pharmacogenomics, the ancestry equity issue, and where the field is going next.
Stretch, get some water, and I'll see you in a few minutes.
